network.overall_strategy: Two experimentally demonstrated prebiotic pathways converge on L-threonine: (1) the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route via lactaldehyde cyanohydrin formation, amination, and nitrile hydrolysis in surface pools, and (2) the Aubrey et al. (2008) hydrothermal aldol condensation of L-alanine with formaldehyde. The network builds from CO2 and H2 in hydrothermal environments to produce C1 building blocks (CO, formaldehyde) and central metabolic hubs (pyruvate), which feed into surface cyanosulfidic chemistry for both lactaldehyde synthesis via the formose-methylglyoxal route and Strecker amino acid synthesis. Five hub molecules (formaldehyde, pyruvate, L-alanine, NH3, CO) create dense interconnections and convergent pathways. Environmental flow proceeds bidirectionally between hydrothermal C1 fixation and surface photochemistry/wet-dry cycling, with final assembly in both environments.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions; serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced to CO via the reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite surfaces. Fe3O4 provides surface sites that activate CO2, facilitating H2-driven reduction. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable at elevated temperatures in hydrothermal systems.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction on iron oxide minerals at hydrothermal conditions is well-established in Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) prebiotic chemistry. CO is a critical C1 intermediate for subsequent carbon fixation. Magnetite is a common hydrothermal mineral catalyst (McCollom & Seewald, 2006; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type formaldehyde synthesis

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis at submarine hydrothermal vents

reactions.mechanism: CO is hydrogenated on magnetite and iron-sulfide mineral surfaces to produce formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) chemistry. CO adsorbs on the mineral surface, is partially hydrogenated, and released as HCHO. This is the first stable oxygenated C1 product of FTT synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from CO and H2 on iron-oxide and iron-sulfide mineral surfaces under hydrothermal conditions is a foundational process in prebiotic carbon fixation. McCollom & Seewald (2007) and Aubrey et al. (2008) identify formaldehyde as a key C1 building block abundantly available in hydrothermal vent fluids from reduction of CO2 and FTT chemistry.

reactions.name: Formose reaction initiation: formaldehyde self-aldol to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions (pH 9-11); borate stabilizes intermediates; warm evaporitic pools

reactions.mechanism: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo aldol condensation to form glycolaldehyde. Under alkaline conditions, one formaldehyde is converted to its enediol form, which attacks the carbonyl of a second formaldehyde. Borate minerals catalyze and stabilize the product against further reactions. This is the rate-limiting initiation step of the formose reaction.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction producing glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde is a cornerstone of prebiotic sugar chemistry (Butlerow, 1861; Breslow, 1959). Borate minerals stabilize the C2 sugar product and prevent runaway polymerization (Ricardo et al., 2004). This reaction is the entry point to prebiotic carbohydrate chemistry from C1 building blocks.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions; borate complexation stabilizes glyceraldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde enolizes under alkaline conditions and the enolate nucleophilically attacks the carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde, forming a new C-C bond and yielding glyceraldehyde (a C3 triose). Borate complexation with the vicinal diol stabilizes the product against degradation and epimerization.
reactions.reasoning: The aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde is a well-characterized step in the formose reaction network. Borate stabilization of sugars was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004). This produces the C3 sugar backbone needed for lactaldehyde synthesis. Corrected from previous attempts: this reaction produces glyceraldehyde (not lactaldehyde), since the aldol places the new hydroxyl at C2-C3 of the triose.

reactions.name: Dehydration of glyceraldehyde to methylglyoxal (pyruvaldehyde)

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling; dehydration during dry phase on clay mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes dehydration/tautomerization to form methylglyoxal (pyruvaldehyde, CH3-CO-CHO) with loss of water. Under dry conditions on montmorillonite surfaces, the triose loses one equivalent of water via an enolization-dehydration sequence. The clay surface catalyzes the dehydration by providing acidic sites.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde readily converts to methylglyoxal under mild heating and dehydration conditions. Methylglyoxal is a known product of sugar degradation under prebiotic conditions (Thornalley, 1996). This transformation is favored during the dry phase of wet-dry cycling on mineral surfaces, a key feature of surface prebiotic environments. Montmorillonite clays provide Brønsted acid sites that catalyze the elimination.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: stibnite (Sb2S3) as photocatalyst and electron source
conditions.other: UV irradiation (200-300 nm); cyanosulfidic conditions with H2S as reductant; selective reduction of keto group preserving aldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Methylglyoxal (CH3-CO-CHO) undergoes selective photoreduction of the keto carbonyl (C2=O) to a hydroxyl group, yielding lactaldehyde (CH3-CHOH-CHO). Under UV irradiation in the presence of sulfide minerals and H2S, photoexcited electrons from stibnite or TiO2 reduce the more electrophilic keto group selectively over the aldehyde. The sulfide serves as sacrificial electron donor, being oxidized to elemental sulfur.
reactions.reasoning: Under cyanosulfidic conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012), UV-driven photoreduction using sulfide minerals can selectively reduce carbonyl groups. Lactaldehyde is described as a product of sugar photochemistry under cyanosulfidic conditions in the Patel et al. (2015) framework. The selective reduction of the keto group over the aldehyde in pyruvaldehyde-type compounds is kinetically favored due to the greater electrophilicity of the keto carbon. Stibnite and TiO2 are documented UV photocatalysts in prebiotic surface chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Ranjan & Sasselov, 2016).

reactions.name: Kiliani cyanohydrin formation from lactaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline pH (8-9) to generate CN⁻ nucleophile; cyanosulfidic pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion (CN⁻) to the aldehyde carbonyl carbon of lactaldehyde. Under mildly alkaline conditions, HCN deprotonates to CN⁻, which attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the aldehyde group in lactaldehyde. Protonation of the resulting alkoxide yields the cyanohydrin (2,3-dihydroxybutanenitrile). This is a classic Kiliani-type reaction that extends the carbon chain from C3 to C4 while introducing a nitrile group.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 1 of the cyanosulfidic threonine synthesis described by Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The cyanohydrin reaction is the initiating step of the Strecker-type amino acid synthesis, using HCN as a C1 nucleophile and chain extender. Phosphate minerals (apatite) serve as pH buffers maintaining the mildly alkaline conditions needed for CN⁻ generation.

reactions.name: Strecker-type amination of cyanohydrin to aminonitrile

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic pool conditions; ammonia from volcanic outgassing or HCN hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Strecker-type amination: ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group at the alpha-carbon adjacent to the nitrile in the cyanohydrin intermediate, converting it to 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile with release of water. NH3 nucleophilically attacks the carbon bearing the alpha-hydroxyl, which acts as a leaving group (as water). The beta-hydroxy group is preserved, maintaining the threonine carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 2 of the cyanosulfidic threonine synthesis described by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The amination converts the cyanohydrin to an aminonitrile, establishing the alpha-amino group characteristic of all amino acids. This Strecker-type amination is a general feature of the cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework. Montmorillonite clay concentrates organics and ammonia on its surface, facilitating the displacement reaction.

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile to L-threonine

conditions.other: Wet phase of wet-dry cycling; mildly acidic to neutral pH promotes hydrolysis; UV irradiation may accelerate

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis: water nucleophilically attacks the nitrile carbon of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile, forming an amide intermediate (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanamide), which further hydrolyzes to the carboxylic acid (L-threonine) with release of ammonia. The overall conversion is C≡N → C(=O)NH2 → C(=O)OH + NH3. This is the terminal step converting the aminonitrile to the final amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 3 of the cyanosulfidic threonine synthesis described by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). Nitrile hydrolysis is the final step of the general Strecker/cyanosulfidic aminonitrile-to-amino acid conversion. The released NH3 is recycled as a nitrogen source for other amination reactions, creating a catalytic nitrogen cycle in the network. Mineral surfaces (montmorillonite, apatite) may facilitate hydrolysis under the aqueous conditions of surface pools.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis via reductive acetyl-CoA pathway analog

conditions.catalyst: Fe0 and greigite (Fe3S4) — iron-sulfur cluster analog
conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions; multi-step process: CO2 → formate → acetate → pyruvate via iron-driven reductive carboxylation; serpentinization-derived H2 as reductant

reactions.mechanism: This is a multi-step process analogous to the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway. (1) CO2 is reduced to formate on greigite surfaces. (2) Formate is further reduced and combined with CO (generated from CO2 via rxn_001) to form an activated acetyl species on FeNiS surfaces, analogous to acetyl-CoA. (3) The acetyl intermediate undergoes reductive carboxylation with a second CO2 to form pyruvate. Greigite (Fe3S4) is a structural and functional analog of the [Fe4S4] clusters in modern acetyl-CoA synthase. Fe0 provides the strong reducing power needed for the carboxylation steps.
reactions.reasoning: Iron-driven CO2 fixation to pyruvate via reductive TCA cycle intermediates was demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) using Fe0 at 100°C. The acetyl-CoA pathway analog on FeNiS minerals was proposed by Huber & Wächtershäuser (1997) and supported by Preiner et al. (2020). This is acknowledged as a multi-step process (not a single-step CO2-to-pyruvate conversion), with greigite providing the catalytic iron-sulfur surfaces and Fe0 providing reducing equivalents. The overall stoichiometry is approximate due to the multiple intermediate steps involved.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to L-alanine

conditions.catalyst: elemental iron (Fe0) as reductant; magnetite surface
conditions.other: pH ~6-8; Fe0 serves as both reductant and catalyst; 70°C demonstrated experimentally by Muchowska et al. (2019)

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination: the keto group of pyruvate reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base) intermediate, which is then reduced by Fe0 to yield L-alanine with release of water. Fe0 provides the two electrons needed for the reduction step. Magnetite surfaces concentrate reactants and facilitate the imine reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated experimentally by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) — Fe0-driven reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 70°C (343K) with hydroxylamine or ammonia as nitrogen source. This is one of the most robust prebiotic amino acid syntheses, producing alanine as one of the most abundant amino acid products. Alanine is a key hub molecule that feeds into the Aubrey et al. (2008) aldol condensation pathway to threonine.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of L-alanine with formaldehyde to L-threonine

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal vent conditions; elevated temperature drives aldol condensation

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type condensation: the alpha-carbon of L-alanine (adjacent to both the amino and carboxyl groups) is deprotonated to form a stabilized carbanion/enolate, which nucleophilically attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde. This forms a new C-C bond and introduces a beta-hydroxyl group (-CH(OH)-), extending the C3 amino acid (alanine) to the C4 amino acid (threonine). The reaction produces the (2S,3R)-threonine stereoisomer along with allo-threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated experimentally by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2). L-alanine reacts with formaldehyde to produce L-threonine under simulated submarine hydrothermal vent conditions. This is directly analogous to the glycine + formaldehyde → serine aldol condensation from the same study. Iron-sulfide minerals (pyrite, mackinawite) are specified as mineral agents providing catalytic surfaces in the hydrothermal environment, consistent with the mineral-rich submarine vent setting described by the authors.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate (CH3-CO-COOH) undergoes thermal decarboxylation, losing CO2 from the carboxyl group to yield acetaldehyde (CH3-CHO). At hydrothermal temperatures, the decarboxylation is kinetically facile. Pyrite surfaces may facilitate the reaction by adsorbing pyruvate and stabilizing the transition state.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of alpha-keto acids under hydrothermal conditions is a well-documented prebiotic process (Cody et al., 2000; Cooper et al., 2011). Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is analogous to the enzymatic pyruvate decarboxylase reaction and occurs spontaneously at elevated temperatures. This reaction connects the pyruvate hub to acetaldehyde, which feeds into the Strecker synthesis of alanine via the surface environment. Pyrite provides catalytic surfaces consistent with the iron-sulfur world hypothesis (Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of 2-aminopropanenitrile from acetaldehyde, NH3, and HCN

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline pH; evaporitic pool conditions; Strecker synthesis — classical prebiotic amino acid precursor route

reactions.mechanism: Classical Strecker synthesis: (1) Acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine (CH3-CH=NH + H2O). (2) HCN (as CN⁻) nucleophilically adds to the imine to form 2-aminopropanenitrile (CH3-CH(NH2)-CN). The overall reaction converts the aldehyde to an alpha-aminonitrile, which is the direct precursor to alanine upon hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the oldest and most well-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1957). Acetaldehyde + NH3 + HCN → 2-aminopropanenitrile is the specific Strecker synthesis for the alanine precursor. This pathway is also part of the cyanosulfidic framework described by Patel et al. (2015), where aminonitrile intermediates are central to amino acid production. Montmorillonite clay provides surface concentration and catalytic sites (Ferris et al., 1996).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 2-aminopropanenitrile to L-alanine

conditions.other: Wet phase of wet-dry cycling; nitrile hydrolysis proceeds via amide intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis of 2-aminopropanenitrile: water attacks the nitrile carbon to form 2-aminopropanamide (alpha-alaninamide), which further hydrolyzes to L-alanine with release of ammonia. The overall conversion is C≡N → C(=O)NH2 → C(=O)OH + NH3. This is the final step of the Strecker amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is the terminal step of the Strecker synthesis and is demonstrated under both thermal and UV-irradiated aqueous conditions (Miller, 1957; Patel et al., 2015). The released ammonia feeds back into other amination reactions (rxn_008, rxn_011, rxn_014), creating an ammonia recycling loop in the prebiotic network. This reaction is also part of the cyanosulfidic aminonitrile-to-amino acid conversion pathway (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry).

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface cyanosulfidic route from CO2
pathways.description: Complete pathway from CO2 through hydrothermal C1 fixation (CO → formaldehyde), surface formose chemistry (formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde → methylglyoxal → lactaldehyde), and cyanosulfidic aminonitrile pathway (lactaldehyde → cyanohydrin → aminonitrile → threonine). Spans all three environments.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal metabolic route: pyruvate → alanine → threonine aldol
pathways.description: Pyruvate is synthesized from CO2/CO/H2 via the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway analog on iron-sulfur minerals. Reductive amination with NH3 and Fe0 produces alanine. Alanine undergoes aldol condensation with formaldehyde to yield threonine. Entirely hydrothermal.

pathways.name: FTT formaldehyde + metabolic alanine → threonine aldol
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis (rxn_001 → rxn_002) combines with alanine from reductive amination of pyruvate (rxn_010 → rxn_011) in the hydrothermal aldol condensation. Demonstrates convergence of two hydrothermal C1 and C3 sources.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation → Strecker alanine → aldol threonine
pathways.description: Pyruvate undergoes thermal decarboxylation to acetaldehyde in hydrothermal environment. Acetaldehyde transfers to surface environment for Strecker synthesis with NH3 and HCN to form 2-aminopropanenitrile, which hydrolyzes to alanine. Alanine returns to hydrothermal environment for aldol condensation with formaldehyde to yield threonine.

pathways.name: Core cyanosulfidic pathway: lactaldehyde → threonine (Patel et al. 2015)
pathways.description: The three-step cyanosulfidic aminonitrile route directly demonstrated by Patel et al. (2015): lactaldehyde + HCN → cyanohydrin → aminonitrile (with NH3) → threonine (via hydrolysis). Operates entirely in surface cyanosulfidic pool conditions.

pathways.name: Formose-to-cyanosulfidic surface pathway
pathways.description: Starting from formaldehyde available in surface pools, the formose reaction builds glycolaldehyde, then glyceraldehyde. Dehydration and photoreduction produce lactaldehyde, which enters the cyanosulfidic aminonitrile pathway to threonine. Entirely surface-based.

pathways.name: Strecker alanine from acetaldehyde → aldol threonine
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde (from hydrothermal decarboxylation or atmospheric chemistry) undergoes Strecker synthesis in surface pools with NH3 and HCN to produce 2-aminopropanenitrile, hydrolyzed to alanine. Alanine then undergoes aldol condensation with formaldehyde in hydrothermal environment to produce threonine.

pathways.name: CO2 → CO → formaldehyde → formose → cyanosulfidic → threonine (complete chain)
pathways.description: The longest pathway tracing carbon from CO2 through the entire network. CO2 is reduced to CO at vents, CO is converted to formaldehyde via FTT, formaldehyde transported to surface for formose chemistry building glyceraldehyde, then dehydrated and photoreduced to lactaldehyde, and finally processed through the full cyanosulfidic aminonitrile pathway to threonine.

pathways.name: Cross-environment convergent: hydrothermal alanine + FTT formaldehyde → threonine
pathways.description: L-alanine synthesized via the hydrothermal iron-catalyzed reductive amination (Muchowska et al., 2019) converges with formaldehyde from FTT synthesis on magnetite. The two products converge in the aldol condensation (Aubrey et al., 2008) to produce threonine. Demonstrates convergence of two independent hydrothermal production streams.

pathways.name: Dual-source alanine convergence: reductive amination + Strecker → aldol threonine
pathways.description: Two independent alanine synthesis routes — hydrothermal reductive amination (rxn_011) and surface Strecker synthesis (rxn_014 → rxn_015) — both produce L-alanine, which converges on the aldol condensation with formaldehyde (rxn_012) to produce threonine. Demonstrates network redundancy and robustness through environmental diversification of a key hub molecule.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent full-chain pathway from CO2 to threonine incorporating both hydrothermal C1 fixation (RWGS, FTT) and surface cyanosulfidic chemistry (Patel et al. 2015). The weakest link is rxn_006 (photoreduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde), which is plausible under cyanosulfidic conditions but the selectivity of reducing one aldehyde carbonyl while retaining the other requires careful conditions. The formose steps (rxn_003, rxn_004) should specify borate or Ca(OH)2 catalysts. The terminal three steps (rxn_007-009) are directly supported by Patel et al. 2015.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalysts (e.g., borate minerals, Ca(OH)2) for formose reactions rxn_003 and rxn_004
Clarify the reductant (H2S) and UV requirement for rxn_006 photoreduction
Consider including the sulfide catalyst explicitly in rxn_006

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short hydrothermal route based on Aubrey et al. 2008. rxn_010 oversimplifies the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway analog into a single step; actual mechanism requires multiple steps and iron/nickel-sulfide catalysts. rxn_011 reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine needs an explicit reducing agent (Fe0 per Muchowska et al. 2019). rxn_012 aldol condensation of alanine with formaldehyde is cited from Aubrey et al. 2008, but the mechanism has subtleties—the alpha-carbon of alanine is already substituted, and the exact stereochemistry/regiochemistry of this aldol is not trivial. Entirely hydrothermal pathway lacks environmental diversity.
suggestions: Break rxn_010 into sub-steps with explicit mineral catalysts (e.g., iron sulfide, awaruite)
Add explicit reducing agent (Fe0) to rxn_011
Discuss threonine thermal stability at hydrothermal temperatures given retro-aldol risk

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent pathway combining FTT formaldehyde production with metabolic alanine synthesis. Still relies on the somewhat problematic rxn_010 and rxn_011 simplifications. However, the convergence of independently produced formaldehyde and alanine at rxn_012 is a nice feature. Both precursors have multiple literature-supported production routes.

Interesting cross-environment pathway: hydrothermal pyruvate → acetaldehyde, then surface Strecker synthesis of alanine, returning to hydrothermal for aldol threonine formation. The pyruvate decarboxylation (rxn_013) is well-established. The Strecker synthesis (rxn_014, rxn_015) is classic prebiotic chemistry. Good environmental bidirectionality. rxn_015 stoichiometry should show 2 H2O consumed.
suggestions: Fix stoichiometry of rxn_015 (needs 2 H2O for nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acid)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Core cyanosulfidic pathway directly from Patel et al. 2015. All three steps are experimentally validated. Kiliani cyanohydrin formation, Strecker amination, and nitrile hydrolysis are well-established under cyanosulfidic surface conditions. This is the strongest pathway in the network. The only limitation is that it assumes lactaldehyde is available as a starting material.
suggestions: This pathway is well-supported; consider noting the cyanosulfidic conditions more explicitly (H2S, UV, phosphate)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Extends P5 by constructing lactaldehyde from formaldehyde via formose chemistry and methylglyoxal photoreduction. The formose steps are well-known but need mineral catalysts specified. The glyceraldehyde → methylglyoxal dehydration (rxn_005) is established. rxn_006 (methylglyoxal photoreduction to lactaldehyde) is the weakest link, requiring selective carbonyl reduction under cyanosulfidic conditions. Good all-surface pathway.
suggestions: Specify borate minerals for formose reaction selectivity
Detail the photoreduction conditions for rxn_006 (wavelength, H2S concentration)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short pathway from acetaldehyde through Strecker alanine to aldol threonine. Combines classic Strecker synthesis (surface) with hydrothermal aldol (Aubrey et al. 2008). The Strecker steps are well-supported. rxn_012 is literature-cited but has mechanistic subtleties regarding the aldol at a fully substituted alpha-carbon.
suggestions: Discuss the regioselectivity of the alanine-formaldehyde aldol more explicitly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Complete chain from CO2 through all environments to threonine. Identical to P1 but framed as CO2 → CO → formaldehyde → formose → cyanosulfidic → threonine. Excellent environmental flow from hydrothermal C1 fixation to surface cyanosulfidic assembly. This is the most complete demonstration of the network's environmental interconnection.
suggestions: Same as P1: specify mineral catalysts for formose steps and clarify rxn_006 conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cross-environment convergent pathway: hydrothermal alanine meets FTT formaldehyde at rxn_012. Nice demonstration of convergent synthesis where two independently produced precursors merge. The convergence point (rxn_012) is well-chosen and literature-supported (Aubrey et al. 2008). Weakened by the oversimplified pyruvate synthesis (rxn_010).
suggestions: Add explicit iron catalyst to rxn_011

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual-source alanine convergence pathway showing two independent routes to alanine (reductive amination and Strecker) that both feed the aldol threonine synthesis. Good demonstration of network redundancy and robustness. The pathway highlights how the network provides multiple backup routes to key intermediates.
suggestions: Clarify which alanine source dominates under realistic conditions
Address whether both alanine routes can operate simultaneously

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network for L-threonine that successfully incorporates both major experimentally demonstrated prebiotic pathways: the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route and the Aubrey et al. (2008) hydrothermal aldol condensation. The network excels in topology (18 molecules, 15 reactions, 5 well-connected hub molecules, 10 pathways with 5 convergence points) and literature alignment (both key papers are faithfully represented). The environmental flow from hydrothermal C1 fixation (RWGS, FTT) through surface cyanosulfidic chemistry is well-designed and bidirectional. Key strengths: (1) multiple convergent routes to the target, (2) hub molecules (formaldehyde, pyruvate, alanine, NH3, CO) create dense interconnections, (3) the lactaldehyde synthesis route addresses a known challenge in the literature (lactaldehyde availability). Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_010 (pyruvate synthesis) is oversimplified into a single step, (2) rxn_011 (reductive amination) lacks an explicit reducing agent, (3) rxn_006 (methylglyoxal photoreduction) needs better specification of conditions, (4) some surface reactions lack mineral catalyst specifications, (5) rxn_012 (alanine + formaldehyde aldol) has mechanistic subtleties regarding regioselectivity at a fully substituted alpha-carbon. The network correctly identifies the chirality problem and threonine thermal stability as open challenges consistent with the literature. Overall, this is a strong, literature-grounded network that merits approval.
